questions.question_text: In Virginia, who typically oversees the real estate settlement process?
questions.correct_answer: An attorney acting as settlement agent
distractors: A title company representative only
The buyer's real estate agent
An escrow officer from a bank
questions.explanation: Virginia is an attorney state where an attorney typically oversees settlements, unlike states such as California where escrow companies handle closings.

questions.question_text: Under TRID rules, who prepares the Closing Disclosure for a Virginia residential closing?
questions.correct_answer: The settlement agent
distractors: The buyer's real estate agent
The local circuit court clerk
The Virginia Real Estate Board
questions.explanation: Under TILA-RESPA Integrated Disclosure (TRID) rules, the settlement agent prepares the Closing Disclosure, which must be provided to the borrower at least three business days before closing.

questions.question_text: A Virginia settlement agent's duties include preparing closing documents and:
questions.correct_answer: Handling fund disbursement after closing
distractors: Negotiating the purchase price for buyers
Inspecting the property before settlement
Approving the buyer's mortgage application
questions.explanation: The settlement agent in Virginia prepares closing documents, ensures all conditions are met, and handles the disbursement of funds to the appropriate parties after closing.

questions.question_text: A buyer relocating from California to Virginia expects an escrow company to handle closing. The agent should explain that Virginia uses:
questions.correct_answer: Attorney-supervised settlement instead
distractors: The same escrow process as California
A title company without attorney oversight
A county government closing officer
questions.explanation: Virginia is an attorney state where settlements are typically overseen by attorneys, unlike California where independent escrow companies commonly handle closings.

questions.question_text: In Virginia, who ensures all closing conditions are met and funds are properly distributed at settlement?
questions.correct_answer: The settlement agent
distractors: The buyer's real estate agent
The listing agent for the seller
The mortgage lender's processor
questions.explanation: In Virginia, the settlement agent (typically an attorney or title company with attorney supervision) verifies all closing conditions, prepares documents, and handles proper disbursement of funds to all parties.

questions.question_text: An owner's title insurance policy in Virginia protects which party?
questions.correct_answer: The buyer against title defects
distractors: The lender against loan default
The seller against future claims
The agent against commission disputes
questions.explanation: An owner's title insurance policy protects the buyer (new owner) against losses from title defects, liens, or encumbrances that existed before the policy date but were not discovered during the title search.

questions.question_text: A Virginia buyer purchases owner's title insurance at closing. This policy protects against losses from:
questions.correct_answer: Title defects existing before the policy date
distractors: Physical damage to the property structure
Future zoning changes by the locality
Mortgage interest rate increases
questions.explanation: An owner's title insurance policy protects the Virginia buyer against losses from undiscovered title defects, liens, or encumbrances that existed before the policy's effective date but were not found during the title search.

questions.question_text: Before closing in Virginia, a title examination is conducted to:
questions.correct_answer: Identify liens, defects, or encumbrances on title
distractors: Determine the property's fair market value
Verify the buyer's creditworthiness
Inspect the physical condition of the home
questions.explanation: A title examination searches public records to identify any liens, defects, or encumbrances that could affect the buyer's ownership rights. This is completed before closing.

questions.question_text: A lender's title insurance policy in Virginia protects the lender up to what amount?
questions.correct_answer: The outstanding loan balance
distractors: The full purchase price of the property
The appraised value at closing
The original loan amount permanently
questions.explanation: A lender's title insurance policy protects the lender's interest up to the outstanding loan balance, which decreases as the borrower pays down the mortgage.

questions.question_text: A title search in Virginia reveals an old mechanics lien. The settlement agent should:
questions.correct_answer: Require the lien to be resolved before closing
distractors: Ignore it if more than one year old
Transfer the lien to the new buyer
Record the deed over the existing lien
questions.explanation: Outstanding liens must be resolved before closing to ensure the buyer receives clear title. The settlement agent works to have liens satisfied or addressed prior to transfer.

questions.question_text: In Virginia, real estate deeds are recorded in the clerk's office of which court?
questions.correct_answer: The circuit court where the property is located
distractors: The state Supreme Court in Richmond
The general district court of the county
The federal district court of the region
questions.explanation: Virginia deeds are recorded in the clerk's office of the circuit court in the jurisdiction where the property is located. Recording provides constructive notice to the public.

questions.question_text: Recording a deed in Virginia provides what type of notice to the public?
questions.correct_answer: Constructive notice
distractors: Actual notice only
Inquiry notice only
No legal notice
questions.explanation: Recording a deed in Virginia's public records provides constructive notice, meaning all parties are legally presumed to know about the recorded interest regardless of whether they actually searched the records.

questions.question_text: Virginia follows a race-notice recording statute. Buyer A purchases first but does not record. Buyer B then purchases and records without knowledge of Buyer A. Who prevails?
questions.correct_answer: Buyer B, who recorded first without notice
distractors: Buyer A, who purchased first in time
Neither, and the property reverts to seller
The court splits ownership equally
questions.explanation: Under Virginia's race-notice statute, a subsequent purchaser who records first and had no notice of the prior unrecorded claim prevails over the earlier purchaser who failed to record.

questions.question_text: Under Virginia's race-notice statute, which two conditions must a subsequent buyer meet to prevail over a prior unrecorded deed?
questions.correct_answer: Record first and have no notice of prior claim
distractors: Pay a higher price and record first
File a court action and obtain title insurance
Obtain a survey and record within 30 days
questions.explanation: Virginia's race-notice statute requires two conditions: the subsequent buyer must (1) record before the prior buyer and (2) have no actual or constructive notice of the prior unrecorded interest.

questions.question_text: A buyer in Virginia does not record the deed after closing. What risk does the buyer face?
questions.correct_answer: A subsequent buyer without notice could prevail
distractors: The deed becomes void after 90 days
The seller can cancel the transaction
Property taxes will not be assessed
questions.explanation: Under Virginia's race-notice statute, failure to record leaves the buyer vulnerable. A later purchaser who buys without notice and records first could obtain superior title.

questions.question_text: Which deed type provides the most protection to a buyer in Virginia?
questions.correct_answer: General warranty deed
distractors: Special warranty deed
Bargain and sale deed
Quitclaim deed
questions.explanation: A general warranty deed provides the most protection because the grantor warrants title against all defects, including those arising before the grantor's ownership, with all statutory covenants.

questions.question_text: A seller conveys property using a special warranty deed. The seller's warranties are limited to:
questions.correct_answer: Defects arising during the seller's ownership
distractors: All defects in the chain of title
Only liens recorded in the past year
Physical defects found during inspection
questions.explanation: A special warranty deed limits the grantor's warranties to the period of the grantor's ownership. The grantor does not warrant against title defects that arose before they acquired the property.

questions.question_text: A family member transfers property using a quitclaim deed in Virginia. This deed provides:
questions.correct_answer: No warranties about the quality of title
distractors: Full warranties against all title defects
Warranties limited to the transfer period
An implied warranty of marketable title
questions.explanation: A quitclaim deed conveys whatever interest the grantor may have without any warranties. It is commonly used for transfers between family members or to clear title clouds.

questions.question_text: A bargain and sale deed in Virginia differs from a quitclaim deed because a bargain and sale deed:
questions.correct_answer: Implies the grantor holds title to the property
distractors: Includes all statutory covenants of title
Requires title insurance before delivery
Must be approved by the circuit court
questions.explanation: A bargain and sale deed contains no express warranties but implies that the grantor holds title. A quitclaim deed makes no such implication and simply conveys whatever interest exists.

questions.question_text: A buyer purchasing a foreclosed property in Virginia receives a special warranty deed. The buyer should be aware that:
questions.correct_answer: Title defects before the seller's ownership are excluded
distractors: The deed provides no protection at all
All prior liens are automatically cleared
The deed guarantees the full chain of title
questions.explanation: A special warranty deed only covers defects during the grantor's period of ownership. For foreclosed properties, title issues predating the foreclosing entity's ownership would not be warranted.
